text: Direct electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water-derived intermediates represents a less hazardous and more sustainable route to propylene oxide production than the current industrial method that utilizes harmful chlorine or peroxide species. Here, an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtOₓ/C) that affords a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% at a current density of 50 mA cm⁻² at ambient pressure is reported. By incorporating platinum into palladium oxide lattice, oxidized platinum species can be stabilized, leading to a superior catalyst stability and performance. The selectivity and stability of the catalyst were confirmed by quantitative kinetic analysis of the epoxidation reaction on PdPtOₓ/C surface, suggesting an electrophilic mechanism that proceeds through the formation of reactive metal-bound peroxo species. In summary, this work provides a sustainable solution to catalyze the selective electrochemical transfer of oxygen atom from water to perform various oxidative transformations in the absence of any catalyst activator.
